text: From ancient times down through considerable stretches of modern exegesis the Epistle of James has been viewed as an invitation to Jews in the Diaspora, regardless of whether they became Christians or not. While this theory is hardly supported by contemporary scholarship, the thesis is worthwhile revisiting the thesis, not the least because it sheds important light on some of the puzzling factors in the letter, including the lack of Christology, a paucity of truly Christian sentiment and the pervasive Jewishness of the text. A Jewish Christian (albeit one who recognized Jesus as Messiah) was interested in having a life-giving missionary program which would enable him to exist in the synagogues and have amicable relations with the Jews who were unwilling to accept Jesus as Messiah. So the writer chose not to make any particular doctrine or item of doctrine the centre of the document, something which he could do in full awareness of numerous parallels from various other parts of the primitive period of Christian history.
headline: Fully Human Written
prediction: We believe that this document is fully human-written
